    # Reading the initialization (config) file may not be enough to set a

    readline_doc = getattr(readline, '__doc__', '')
    if readline_doc is not None and 'libedit' in readline_doc:
        readline.parse_and_bind('bind ^I rl_complete')

By default the web interface will use the same ip/domain and port 6680 for it's initial Mopidy connection. For example if your mopidy server was `music.desktop` then the web interface would connect to `music.desktop:6680`.

The only time I think this wouldn't work out of the box is if your webserver was hosted on a different machine to the mopidy server (ie `spotmop.laptop` as web interface and `mopidy.desktop` as mopidy server). In this instance the `~/.config/mopidy/mopidy.config` file will not be relevant. The solution that comes to mind is to have an additional web interface config file, but this isn't a desirable solution.

Here are several tips:
1. You obviously need python to be installed. I used version 3.6
2. I couldn't use fusesoc from cygwin
3. You must be able to run `git` from windows shell of your choise (cmd/powershell) before installing fusesoc. I had success with https://git-for-windows.github.io/
4. When installing fusesoc you should run shell with admin privileges 
5. You_may_ need to update pbr and setuptools using `pip install -U pbr setuptools_scm` command
6. Make sure that all your building tools are in widows `PATH` environment variable

With this I was able to install fusesoc from git using `pip install -e .` using powershell and perform `build` command. For me it's really good news as now I can show fusesoc to my students in class with windows PCs.

Hi, there are two different timeout in ldap3:
* **connect_timeout**: timeout in seconds for the connect operation, defined in the Server object
* **receive_timeout**: set the socket in non-blocking mode - raising an exception after the specified amount of seconds if nothing is received over the wire, defined in the Connection object


The first one is used when opening to socket to the server, the second one is used in every communication after the connection has been established, so I think you should try to change the latter.

After some more digging, it looks like libjpeg-turbo does in fact support reading RGBA pixel types, but the alpha channel is always guaranteed to be set to 255.  Writing RGBA JPEG images also seems to be supported, but I haven't been able to get that to work.

Relevant comments from libjpeg-turbo source:
https://github.com/libjpeg-turbo/libjpeg-turbo/blob/a0b7de9a47358a19b31db447dbf02b488463eb7e/jpeglib.h#L231-L236

Neither the standard libjpeg included with dlib nor the most recent version appear to have any support for the RGBA color space whatsoever.

So, a symmetric write/read capability doesn't seem to be in cards at the moment.  Not failing when loading an image that has 4 planes still seems worthwhile, though.  At least helps me out, since I ran across a 4-plane image in an "in the wild" dataset that I needed to process.  Unfortunately, despite my best efforts, I have been unable to save my own 4 channel JPEG image and the one that I have I cannot distribute.

One solution is to consider alignments on the concept/node-type to be analyzed as alignments on the node. That is, when you have:

    (a /abc :ARG0 (b / bcd~e.1) ...

The alignment is placed on `(a, ARG0, b)`. Then, whichever variable is expanded to a full node gets the alignment serialized on the concept. If you only change the loop to i <= 60 then at a minimum you could wait a total of 367.3228 seconds before you get to the exception (assuming no data is being read).  On top of the wait time, specifying 60 doesn't really give an indicator of how long you are actually waiting until the exception is thrown.

If you set i += main_delay then your i <= 60 is based off of actual wait time.  Example of setting i += main_delay, let's say main_delay is set to 3, on your first iteration through the loop you will be waiting 3.3 seconds, i now reflects the actual wait time.  Your next iteration, main_delay will equal 3.63, however i will equal 6.93 which reflects actual waiting time.  Hence, now i <= 60 reflects time in seconds of waiting.  I personally like this approach better and that's why I suggested I could add an auth_timeout variable so we can make it i <= self.auth_timeout, instead of i <= 60.  This gives the user flexibility if their environment takes awhile for authentications.

@jftuga You don't want to send `configure` as a command, you need to use the other method on net_connect, `send_config_set`, to get into configuration mode, Example:

```
config_list = ['set interface ethernet eth1 blah']
net_connect.send_config_set(config_list)
```

If you just want to view config that's when you'd use `net_connect.send_command()`:

```
command_list = ['terminal length 9999','show configuration all']
net_connect.send_config_set(command_list)
```

If you use `send_config_set` it has handling for `$` vs `#` built in.

I will update the documentation making sure all examples are correct. Keep in mind that the order of exceptions is important. `Exception` is the most generic one, therefore it needs to be last.

```
from pebble import ProcessPool                                               
from pebble.common import ProcessExpired                                     
from concurrent.futures import TimeoutError                                  
                                                                             
def function(foo, bar=0):                                                    
    return foo + bar                                                         
                                                                             
elements = list(range(1000))                                                 
                                                                             
with ProcessPool() as pool:                                                  
    iterator = pool.map(function, elements, timeout=10)                      
    while True:                                                              
        try:                                                                 
            results = next(iterator)                                         
            print(results)                                                   
        except TimeoutError as error:                                        
            print("Function took longer than %d seconds" % error.args[1])    
        except ProcessExpired as error:                                      
            print("%s. Exit code: %d" % (error, error.exitcode))             
        except StopIteration:                                                
            break     
        except Exception as error:                                           
            print("function raised %s" % error)                              
            print(error.traceback)  # Python's traceback of remote process   
```

The line:

    per.writeCharacteristic(14,bytes('aa','utf-8'))

is going to write the two bytes 0x61 0x61 to the characteristic, because `bytes(string,encoding)` turns a string into a block-of-bytes containing its ASCII (or UTF-8) encoding, and 0x61 is the ASCII encoding of 'a'.

You could use any of the following:
* `binascii.a2b_hex('aa')`   (you should `import binascii` somewhere)
* `bytes([0xAA])`
* `struct.pack('<B', 0xAA)` (use `import struct` beforehand)

Hope this helps,
Ian

@fpischedda  As an example using numpy.linspace:  In plain python,  you'll see that
```python
from numpy import linspace
linspace(1, 100, 1e3)    # no noticeable delay
linspace(1, 100, 1e6)    # slight delay, not bad if that's what you need to do
linspace(1, 100, 1e9)    # noticably slow
linspace(1, 100, 1e12)   # probably take all day
``` 
Even in python, Ctrl-C will take a long time to interrupt this call.   The thing is, this happens in C, and in a single call.  In asteval, it happens all within the call to the actual function (`linspace`) inside `Interpreter.on_call()`.  

Similarly, 
```python
x =9**5**5   # < 0.1 sec 
y =5**9**9   #  > 10 sec 
```
It's very hard for the Interpreter itself to say "I've been running this function too long", because execution never actually gets back to the Interpreter at all until the function is complete.  You really need something monitoring the process.    And the examples show that there's essentially no way for the interpreter to predict run time.

But, I think that is what you really care about anyway -- not so much that the evaluation of a single node is taking too long, but that the process is hung.

1. You may lengthen the `timeout` (given to `ChatHandler`) to 3 hours or however long you want it to be. But this will keep alive a lot of "dormant" threads where the user has left but timeout has not been reached. I don't recommend this solution.

2. You may save the `ChatHandler`'s state just before it dies, by overriding its `on_close` method. Either save it in memory, in a file, or in a database. Retrieve those same information in the constructor, so whenever the same user makes contact again, he will have a `ChatHandler` with the same state as when he fell asleep 3 hours before.

3. Put some checking in `on_callback_query` to tell the user that his session has expired and "please send me a message to start over" or something like that. This way, you don't have to save state, but still make sure the user is doing what he is supposed to do.

As a general rule, you should always keep track of the stage a user is in, and check whether he is acting as expected. In your example, when you are expecting a callback query from him, he may send you 3 more chat messages in a row. You have to consider that, if you are making a serious application.

```
    match = [{"index": 0, "mode": "src", "inverse": True}]
    ip.tc("add-filter", "basic", ifb0, em_kind="ipset",

    ip.tc("add-filter", "basic", ifb0, em_kind="ipset",
            parent=0x10000, classid=0x10010, match=match) 
   # some black magic in ematch-ipset module convert name to index

"DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT" is simply an alias. One could do what you want by zeroing the fields (beware that it's a hierarchical set of structures).

This should work:

for entry in pe.DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT:
    entry.struct.Characteristics = 0
    entry.struct.TimeDateStamp = 0
    entry.struct.ForwarderChain = 0
    entry.struct.Name = 0
    entry.struct.FirstThunk = 0

That will set all the import entries, one per DLL to zero. You can then write the results and the import descriptors should be gone. Note that the import tables will still be somewhere in the file, unreferenced now.

Ok, I put together a workaround now (or maybe this is how it's intended) with tzlocal and pytz that seems to work.
```
from datetime import datetime
import parsedatetime as pdt
import sys
import pytz

# http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13218506/how-to-get-system-timezone-setting-and-pass-it-to-pytz-timezone
from tzlocal import get_localzone # $ pip install tzlocal
local_tz = get_localzone()

def parse_args(argv=sys.argv):
    cal = pdt.Calendar()
    timestr = ' '.join(argv)
    # ret = cal.parseDT(timestr, sourceTime=datetime.utcnow(), tzinfo=pytz.utc)[0]
    ret = cal.parseDT(timestr, tzinfo=local_tz)[0]
    ret_utc = ret.astimezone(pytz.utc)
    print(ret)
    print(ret_utc)

    ret_loc = ret_utc.astimezone(local_tz)
    print(ret_loc)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parse_args()
```

You can certainly deploy an application developed that uses easygui!

How you do so very much depends on the type of application/deployment you want to do. One of the standard ways to do this would be to use [setuptools](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html). Specifically you may want to consider building a [wheel](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#wheels) for distribution. You can then copy the wheel across to the target system and use `easy_gui` to install it (presuming that you have a python installation on the target system already).

Easygui is available through the Python Package Index (PyPi) so you can specify it as a required dependency in your setup.py, and do not need to manually include any easygui files.

If you're reading around on this, it is worth noting that there are a *lot* of ways of doing deployment. 
Within the python world, `setuptools` is the successor to `distutils`, and the `wheel` packaging system is the successor to the `egg`. Wherever you see those terms you know that they are talking about the same sort of thing.

Hope this is useful! I'd suggest that you have a look into using `setuptools` to build a `wheel`, and let me know if you have further questions.

> I suppose one question is do we always want to do this (also update the default) during deprecation if it is happening or is this something that may not be wanted. Really a few more use-cases would help with my thinking here. Anyhow the main thing is that we can easily do this the difficulty is about deciding how and when we do it.

If we look at the sysdata case, I think the `update_placeholder_format` could be in the Meta class but in `updated` instead of `deprecated`. `updated` would be run as a pre-post-config. In our case, that means in `deprecated` we can replace the old `*_threshold` with the new `threshold` and set the `precision` and `padding`. Then, in `updated`, we can set the default `precision` and `padding`. That way, there are three cases:
1. The user used the old `padding`/`precision`. They are set during the deprecation stage.
2. The user used the placeholders but didn't specify padding or precision. We set it to its default.
3. The user used placeholders with padding and precision. We don't do anything.

For naming clashes there are at least 3 options:

1. Ignore. This would be used if the names in the source IDF are deliberately the same as in the target IDF. For example, you may use a common set of materials/construction names in your surfaces, and then as part of your workflow import the constructions from an IDF used as a materials library.
2. Suffixes. Pass a suffix to append to names from the source IDF wherever there are clashes.
3. Fail. Return a list of clashes so the user can either manually resolve them, or choose one of the other methods of resolving clashes.

For spatial clashes there are at least three options:

1. Intersect/match. Treat the clash as intnentional. For example, a clash may occur if the new geometry adjoins existing geometry.
2. Offset. Move the new geometry by a vector such that it doesn't clash. This could be used for laying out up a campus of buildings from individual IDFs.
3. Fail. Produce a report on the clash and fail gracefully, suggesting the other methods as 
possible resolutions.

I took a look at this problem and it's quite ugly... 

First of all, Flask-Babelex was forked from Flask-Babel a while ago, and as of now, it's "75 commits behind python-babel:master".  I believe there are a few folks out there who is looking to migrate from Flask-babelex back to Flask-Babel. (see https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/issues/715) 

Flask-Babelex was designed to be backwards compatible to Flask-Babel. Hence, both Flask-Babelex and Flask-Babel uses the same name when being registered to Flask (both Flask-Babelex and Flask-Babel uses current_app.extensions['babel']). 

This causes a problems because Flask-User uses Flask-Babel which references current_app['babel'] which in turn is referencing to Flask-Babelex instantiated by Flask-Admin.

@cdrlee My solution is monkey patching Flask-Babelex's Babel variable.

This is working for me: 
```
  babel = Babel(app)
  # Monkeypatching Flask-babelex
  babel.domain = 'flask_user'
  babel.translation_directories = 'translations'
```
To make this work, *your translation folder must be located at `APP/APP/translations`. See `flask_babelex/__init__.py` 's `get_translation` and `domain` variable. 

@lingthio  I don't think it's worth the trouble to get Flask-User work on Flask-Babelex as it is an outdated package. Instead, I believe Flask-Admin should move back to Flask-Babel as the the latter has added functionalities to deprecate the need for Flask-Babelex.
Or (better) we can improve existing project `mimesis-cloud`.

Hi,

Since you provided no code, but this is an easy one: a test is worth a thousand words.

The dataset used is in the sources of `backtrader`

```python
import random

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds


class St(bt.Strategy):
    def __init__(self):
        self.order = None

    def notify_order(self, order):
        if order.status in [order.Completed]:
            dtstr = bt.num2date(order.executed.dt).isoformat()
            if order.isbuy():
                print('BUY EXECUTED, on:', dtstr)
            else:  # Sell
                print('SELL EXECUTED on:', dtstr)

    def next(self):
        if not random.randint(0, 5):  # roll a dice to decide entering/exit
            dtstr = self.data.datetime.datetime().isoformat()
            if self.position:
                print('SELL CREATED, on:', dtstr)
                self.close(exectype=bt.Order.Close)
            else:
                print('BUY CREATED, on:', dtstr)
                self.buy(exectype=bt.Order.Close)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()

    data = btfeeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname='2005-2006-day-001.txt')
    cerebro.adddata(data)
    cerebro.addstrategy(St)
    cerebro.run()
```

Output (not everything, it would be too long)

```
BUY CREATED, on: 2005-01-06T23:59:59
BUY CREATED, on: 2005-01-07T23:59:59
BUY EXECUTED, on: 2005-01-07T23:59:59
BUY EXECUTED, on: 2005-01-10T23:59:59
SELL CREATED, on: 2005-01-13T23:59:59
SELL EXECUTED on: 2005-01-14T23:59:59
BUY CREATED, on: 2005-01-19T23:59:59
BUY EXECUTED, on: 2005-01-20T23:59:59
SELL CREATED, on: 2005-02-22T23:59:59
SELL EXECUTED on: 2005-02-23T23:59:59
...
...
BUY CREATED, on: 2006-12-14T23:59:59
BUY CREATED, on: 2006-12-15T23:59:59
BUY EXECUTED, on: 2006-12-15T23:59:59
BUY EXECUTED, on: 2006-12-18T23:59:59
SELL CREATED, on: 2006-12-19T23:59:59
SELL EXECUTED on: 2006-12-20T23:59:59
```

Orders are getting created on day `d0` and being executed on day `d1`, which is not necessarily the next calendar day, because weekends play a role. The last lines contain an example: Dec 15th 2006 was a Friday and the order gets executed on the next trading day: Mon, Dec 18th 2006.

The overlapping trades is a side effect of the `Close` order type. You issue an order and will be executed at the end of next day. But you can go in next day and decide to buy again, before you actually have a position.

This is because the logic used to buy and sell is rolling a dice (random integer [0, 5] delivering a 0) and it can kick in during several consecutive days). With a *sentinel* in place (checking if an order is pending) that behavior can be avoided.

@sbliven this uses the `chpwd_functions` feature of `zsh` to integrate with `cd`. If you're using `bash`, `rvm` has a small bash "library" that was extracted into [`mpapis/bash_zsh_support`](https://github.com/mpapis/bash_zsh_support) which allows these `cd` overrides to be defined using the same `chpwd_functions` that's available in `zsh`. Loading either `avn` or `rvm` before defining the above would get you that interop "library".

`autoenv` does not use `chpwd_functions`, but feel free to 👍  #92. This little hack above is _not_ a replacement to sourcing `activate.sh`. You should add it after sourcing that. It unsets the override of `cd` after defining a function to [execute the necessary `autoenv` stuff](https://github.com/kennethreitz/autoenv#disclaimer) during the `chpwd` hook.

I like the idea but as I was working it through, I think there's a better option

Not all json modules (especially `ujson`, `yajl` and other C/C++-based modules) support the `cls` kwarg, that's why `_dump_json` needs some kind of guard. But they do often support other arguments.
 
Which actually makes me think that instead of doing this specific argument, it would make more sense to do some kind of `JSON_{LOAD|DUMP}_OPTIONS` setting(s) that let users specify any number of kwargs to the `{module}.loads` and `{module}.dumps` methods, regardless of the module. It could even default to `{'cls': django.core.serializers.json.DjangoJSONEncoder}` to keep the current default of using Django's encoder. People like me who opt for the faster, less flexible encoders could just set it to `None`, then `_dump_json` would do something like `if OPTIONS is not None: return json.dumps(obj, **OPTIONS); return json.dumps(obj)`.

Let me know if this solves your question: in lifelines, all the processing & encoding is done outside of lifelines. That is, before the dataframe even hits lifelines, it should be encoded as you wish. So it's up to you whether the baseline is control or treatment in the dataframe column. The inference should proceed as normal, and the resulting `cph.print_summary` should represent the choice chosen. 

Popular encoding functions are available in the _patsy_ library (which has a R-style formula system) and Pandas `get_dummies` function (but this doesn't allow you wish to chose as the baseline. 

```python
df = pd.DataFrame.from_records([
  {'t': 1, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'control'},
  {'t': 2, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'treatment'},
  {'t': 3, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'treatment'},
  {'t': 4, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'control'},
  {'t': 5, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'treatment'},
  {'t': 6, 'e': 0, 'cata': 'control'},
])

df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns='cata')
# choice is now yours to make either baseline, so either:
del df['cata_control']
# or 
del df['cata_treatment']

cph = CoxPHFitter()
cpf.fit(df, 't', 'e')
...

```

Hello,
Thank you for replying.

`django-rest-knox` documentation does state that it is based on or is similiar to `TokenAuthentication` from the `django-rest-framework` -- which makes use of a json payload to authenticate. The `django-rest-framework-jwt` project does the same thing and supports http basic auth aswell -- whereas `django-rest-knox` just support http basic auth. Coming from both of those frameworks, I was surprised to
learn that `django-rest-knox` only uses http basic auth which I have come to expect with this `batteries included` concept of the Python world.

However in terms of the actual difference between http basic auth via header vs. doing it with a json payload -- there are no differences in terms of security or performance, heck one could argue that http basic auth is the most widely supported one in terms of libraries.

So my main motivation for this PR was more or less just because other frameworks support it and I wanted to even the gap between those.

### Reference documentation
* [DRF -- TokenAuthentication](https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#generating-tokens)
* [DRF-JWT -- Usage](https://getblimp.github.io/django-rest-framework-jwt/#Usage)

```
# use little-endian hex to bytes
from bitcoin.core import lx, b2lx, x, b2x
import bitcoin.rpc
proxy = bitcoin.rpc.Proxy()
txid = "269b22e789089b3acfef5f118a5b2c9856a70833756594c2c2a16f070b830147"
proxy._call("gettransaction", txid)
proxy.gettransaction(lx(txid))
```

In python 2.7,

```
proxy.gettransaction("269b22e789089b3acfef5f118a5b2c9856a70833756594c2c2a16f070b830147")
```

... should cause an error like "Invalid or non-wallet transaction id (-5)". However, in python 3 the same line should cause an error like "TypeError: 'str' does not support the buffer interface".

Note how `b2lx` (used in `gettransaction`) does not complain about receiving a hex encoded string value in python 2.7. Test with `b2lx("269b22e789089b3acfef5f118a5b2c9856a70833756594c2c2a16f070b830147")` to see the difference between python versions. Your problem is data mangling and using ancient python.

Use `gettransaction(lx(txid))`.

Perhaps that the most direct and efficient solution for you is to edit the default template(or create a new one) to add the code you need in the preamble. For the `jupyter nbconvert --to latex_with_lenvs` conversion, the default template is `thmsInNb_article.tplx` which is located in the templates subdirectory of the latex_envs directory. The exact location depends on your system and the way you installed the extension. For me, it is here: `~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/latex_envs/templates/thmsInNb_article.tplx` In this file, you should add your code in the `((* block commands *))` section. If you want to add your own template, put it in the directory and call the conversion as `jupyter nbconvert --to latex_with_lenvs --template newtemplate.tplx`.
Note that if you use a `\def` like the one above for `\sha` you would need to remove it before compiling in order to avoid conflict between the two definitions.

A couple things:
- The Spark Standalone instructions were intended for a simple single-node "cluster".  I'd try running the instructions on a single-node first (if you haven't done so already).
- If you have Hadoop/YARN installed, you should probably follow the [YARN instructions](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/wiki/GetStarted_YARN).  There are important bits to allow TF to read from HDFS.
- If you still want to use a Spark Standalone Cluster (w/o YARN), then you will need to ensure the `libhdfs.so` path is set per the YARN instructions, and you should probably use a fully-qualified path to the model directory, e.g. `hdfs://default/user/${USER}/mnist_model`.
- And, if that doesn't work for you, please see if there are any errors in the executor logs (via Spark UI Executors tab, or via `yarn logs -applicationId <your_appId>`.

Hope that helps...

Having dealt with integrating the edge-triggered FD in an eventloop in zmq.green, I don't want to be responsible for maintaining another implementation that relies on it. I've pushed for a level-triggered separate read/write FD API in libzmq for years, so if that ever happens I will happily drop the `zmq_poll` implementations and use it.

Fortunately, aiozmq exists and takes on that challenge, so please feel free to use that. I don't view zmq.asyncio as a replacement for it at all, just one simple implementation of AIO-compatible use of zmq.

I missed this issue when it came in.  This seems like a reasonable request but any explicit support is something I'd have to think about more.

Rather than monkeypatch, you can supply your own logging function to `backoff` via event handlers https://github.com/litl/backoff#event-handlers  

You could imitate the default logger implementations [here](https://github.com/litl/backoff/blob/88c9ec803366bd092d88acaed699203359a129a3/backoff/_common.py#L67) and have them use your adapter instead of the default backoff logger.

I actually have a feeling that it may be due to the fact that your cloudsearch is the old version of cloudsearch ``'2011-02-01'`` because after looking at the docs: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudsearch/latest/developerguide/migrating.html there is a lot of functionality changes. We are currently calling 2013-01-01 operations and based on the docs it seems that the 2011-02-01 API is needed to interact with 2011-02-01 domains. You can probably check by registering a handler to use the old version (note that I cannot confirm this because I am unable to create domains of that version. It is a legacy thing):
```py
import boto3

client = boto3.client('cloudsearch', 'us-east-1')
def switch_api_version(request, **kwargs):
    request.data['Version'] = '2011-02-01'

client.meta.events.register('before-sign.cloudsearch', switch_api_version)
client.describe_index_fields(DomainName='cloudsearchdomain1')
```

You may need to look at processing your tiles in batches. I'm not sure about other possible workarounds.

thanks @timrogers.  I was just reviewing the code, and I think there may be a serious issue which may result in duplicate resources being created under rare conditions.  It looks like in `BaseService. _perform_request`, POST requests are retried up to 3 times when an error is encountered.  The issue I see is that the `Idempotency-Key` is generated within `ApiClient.post`, which means the `Idempotency-Key` will be different per attempt.  So if the first attempt fails with an error, it will be retried with a different `Idempotency-Key`.  I think the generation of the `Idempotency-Key` needs to be moved to within `BaseService. _perform_request` to ensure all attempted POST requests use the same key.

I made a big update. See the latest commit message:

```
Adds a group_by. Gets rid of ExcitedColumns and DepolarizedCells.

This is doing fundamentally the same thing, but it's now described
using a reusable concept: the "group by".

The code is more verbose, but there's less conceptual overhead that's
specific to the Temporal Memory. And it encourages better practices --
the previous code would encourage you to reuse the ExcitedColumns and
DepolarizedCells even when you only cared about some of the sequences
that it traversed (e.g. just the active segments).

According to temporal_memory_performance_benchmark.py, this change is
performance-neutral.
```

It makes this PR bigger, but it saves you the hassle of having to read my previous code (e.g. the `DepolarizedCells` class which was the cell equivalent of the `ExcitedColumns` class). And it makes `ExtendedTemporalMemory.cpp` ~400 lines smaller.

Hey everyone, when I `pip install "qtpy>1.3"` and set `QT_API=pyqt4`, the only attributes available in the  `QtCore.QStandardPaths` module are the data attributes for the locations the class/enum `StandardLocation` and `displayName(...)`.  Other methods defined by QStandardPaths are missing, most notably the methods that are really helpful for transforming standard paths to actual paths such as `QtCore.QStandardPaths.writableLocation(...)`.

The temporary workaround is to `pip install "qtpy<1.3"`.

To reproduce, compare:
* `QT_API=pyqt4 python -c "from qtpy import QtCore; help(QtCore.QStandardPaths)"`
* `QT_API=pyqt5 python -c "from qtpy import QtCore; help(QtCore.QStandardPaths)"`

Hi @jxrossel, to start Spyder on debugging mode you must need to set the ``SPYDER_DEV`` variable, as it follows, the following commands must be executed from a system terminal, which corresponds to ``cmd.exe`` on Windows:

```batch
set SPYDER_DEV="True"
spyder
```
After the execution, the file ``spyder_terminal_err.log`` must appear on your current working directory, which is this case must be the directory of the current terminal. Please post the contents of this file, so we can help you to trace the error.

Actually we've discussed something similar in #199 . I think the pain point here is that `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns` repeats the slow forward return computation every time.  So I simply split out this part and reuse everything else.

I make this `create_factor_data` function based on `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`, where computing forward returns is replaced with pre-computed values, and in the signature `price` is replaced with `forward_returns`, `periods ` removed. 
```Python
def create_factor_data(factor,
                       forward_returns,
                       groupby=None,
                       by_group=False,
                       quantiles=5,
                       bins=None,
                       groupby_labels=None):

    # Replace calling compute_forward_returns with pre-computed values
    merged_data = forward_returns.copy() 

    # Keep everything else unchanged below
    ......
```

Now the analysis code becomes something like:
```
periods = (20, 40, 60, 120)
forward_returns = alphalens.utils.compute_forward_returns(prices=pricing, periods=periods)

factor_data_1 = create_factor_data(
    factor=factor_1, 
    forward_returns=forward_returns,
    quantiles=10
)

factor_data_2 = create_factor_data(
    factor=factor_2, 
    forward_returns=forward_returns,
    quantiles=10
)

I find reusing the pre-computed forward returns saves much time, as I often test different factors with the same set of forward returns.

I _think_ there is an error in the C# encoding of int32s. When I serialize an int32 with value -1 in protobuf, the resulting bytes are [8, 255, 255, 255, 255, 255, 255, 255, 255, 1]. It looks like the bytes in your serialization are corresponding to that value are [08, 255, 255, 255, 255, 15]. The first one seems to be correct according to protobuf doc: `  If you use int32 or int64 as the type for a negative number, the resulting varint is always ten bytes long – it is, effectively, treated like a very large unsigned integer. `https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/encoding?hl=en

@MalcolmSun @neubig Just to give my understanding : 

I think you should not consider the embedding dimension as the "second dimension" of the convolution. 

To me it is more like the number of channels that you have in an image (R, G, B, alpha,...), or like a "depth" and you only "shift" the filter along 1 dimension : the time dimension

So

- 1D dimension = filters have the shape [temporal span]x[number of channels] and you only shift the filters along the time dimension (1D)

- 2D dimension = filters have the shape [width]x[height]x[number of channels] and you shift the filter along the width and height dimension of the 2D input (image,...)

etc...

Hey @jpalanco, 

Regarding your issue, from what I understood you want all the rows in your table that contain `element_of_list_1`. Your **First case** is wrong because it tries to apply any() to an expression. 

And The **second case** is applying .contains() to a list and it's translated in SQL as :

```
SELECT resource_a.id AS resource_a_id, resource_a.timestamp AS resource_a_timestamp,
 resource_a.object_a AS resource_a_object_a 
 FROM resource_a 
 WHERE ((resource_a.object_a['list_1']) LIKE 'element_of_list_1' + ? || 'element_of_list_1');
```

This is wrong because resource_a.object_a['list_1'] is a list so it can't be cast into a string.

I think what you are looking for is : 

```
select * from resource_a
where 'element_of_list_1' = any(resource_a.object_a['list_1']);
```

which in your script should look like this : 

```
from sqlalchemy import any_
...
expr = 'element_of_list_1' == any_(ResourceA.object_a['list_1'])
results = DBSession.query(ResourceA).filter(expr ).all()
```

The right answer for this under Twisted is "use Crochet" (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/crochet). I don't know if there's a similar thing for asyncio.

Also, txaio isn't really for "client" applications -- it's for libraries to reduce the amount of code required to present a "twisted" **or** an "asyncio" API to users. However, users of this library still have to choose one or the other. So, if they chose "Twisted" but wanted synchronous code they'd just use Crochet.

config = ComponentConfig("abc", {})
session = ApplicationSession(config)
runner.run(session)
```

> I'm not sure I'm seeing any problems with gp2 SSD volumes... should I really be considering using ephemeral volumes for the etcd journal?

We saw tons of etcd leader election thrashing before, and we remedied this by moving the etcd data volume to the fast local disk and using a separate EBS for Docker. We suspect that the IO to the Docker volume was preventing etcd from syncing to disk often enough. 

If you're not seeing issues, I wouldn't be too concerned. This was all before we switched the EBSes over to gp2, so that could alleviate this as well (although separating etcd does seem to be a best practice).

I'd hit the etcd stats endpoint to see if it reports hitting any heartbeat timeouts on its peers.

Ok, I have seen your changes in feature_extraction :+1:

> Hi, I am having a time series data, I have the target predictive variable and around 100 independent variables. 

are you trying to do time series forecasting or classification?

Good questions:

> 1. What is unique about the feature filtering ? for example, let's say I want to use the features for classification. Can't I just take all the features to an information.gain software (like FSelector in R), it will tell me how good/bad each feature is, based on information gain, and by this way to use the feature ?

Its based on classical statistical hypothesis tests and a multiple test procedure. This means that the percentage of irrelevant among the extracted features will be asymptotically controlled by the filtering. 
Basically we take vintage statistical methods to solve a machine learning problem.

Most other feature selection algorithms such as the Boruta do not give you any insights in how many good or bad features they filter out. Often the algorithms "just work". With FRESH it is different. There are some more important points, just look in the paper for more details.

> 2. You have a long list of features, from your experience, how much is left after feature filtering ? I know it is a very general question, and it is very time series specific, but I wonder, from your experience, the typical filtering.

From our experience the selected features are often a mixture between basic features (mean, median) and very specialized features such as certain wavelet coefficient. The selection highly depends on the domain though. There is now "almighty feature"

> 3. What is the "smart thing" about the tsfresh solution ? Is it new features, new filtering method, combo, please epxlain ?

I updated the CommentedSeq class to support moving of comments on `.insert()` and `.pop()`.
in version 0.10.23 of ruamel.yaml.

The tests are [here](https://bitbucket.org/ruamel/yaml/src/90e4fb18d899b2a10de7138f77641ebbc0035399/_test/test_comments.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#test_comments.py-284). I think that should solve your issue, let me know if it doesn't.

Please not that comments hang off sequence items and any blank line after the last item in a sequence is part of that last comment. So inserting at the end, or appending, will result in the blank line being between the last item (just inserted) and the one before that. This is IMO not solvable without some better semantics as to when stuff (comments/blank lines) belongs to the previous (last scalar) parsed, the previous toplevel construct or the next construct, or its first item.

This may be the case if you created the snapshots with something other than Curator, and they have a timestamp in the name that differs from what Curator assigns by default.

You'll note that the `--timestring` definition for snapshots differs from the one for indices:

### Snapshots
The code which yields this is here: https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/cli/snapshot_selection.py#L24-L25
```
--timestring TEXT               Python strftime string to match your
                                  snapshot's definition, e.g. 20140715020304
                                  would be %Y%m%d%H%M%S
```


### Indices
The code which yields this is here: https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/cli/index_selection.py#L23-L24
```
--timestring TEXT               Python strftime string to match your index
                                  definition, e.g. 2014.07.15 would be
                                  %Y.%m.%d
```

Note that there is a default value for snapshots, but no default for indices.  This is likely why you had no matches.  I don't think an update to the code is necessary here.  I _could_ add `show_default=True` to that line, to indicate that there is a default timestring, but that would make long output even longer.  Education and documentation are perhaps better solutions.

Hey @lmazuel 

Thanks for the response! Just skimming over ADAL it looks like I could get this to work well with my use case. As far as implementation goes, if I don't want to wait would modifying the Azure Python SDK require a sizable code change? Is this something that could be easily completed in my own fork?

@annatisch / @lmazuel  Let me know if you have any feedback or if this is good to merge.

In case anyone else runs into issues with `add_data_disk`:

The way to get the errors for async operations, such as `add_data_disk`, is to call `get_operation_status`:
```
result = service.add_data_disk(service_name, deployment_name, role_name, lun, None, None, label, None, None, url)

# later, check periodically to see how the async operation is doing
op_result = service.get_operation_status(result.request_id)
print(op_result.status)
print(vars(op_result.error))
```

`.status` will be `InProgress`, `Succeeded`, or something else in case of an error.

The fact that `add_data_disk` is async and `add_disk` isn't is somewhat unfortunate, and out of our control in the python sdk (the REST api is designed that way).

In the unit tests, we have a helper function that blocks (_wait_for_async), which you can adapt for your needs, if blocking is what you want:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/tests/test_servicemanagementservice.py#L195

In the future, it would be nice to take advantage of Python 3.4's asyncio.

One thing we can do for now is clearly mark which operations are async and which aren't in the documentation comments for each method.

No, I don't think so, this should be fixed on the server side.

According to RFC, a HTTP request can have multiple responses, with only the last one be 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, or 5xx. There can be any number of 1xx responses before the final response.

https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#page-50

```
6.2.  Informational 1xx

   The 1xx (Informational) class of status code indicates an interim
   response for communicating connection status or request progress
   prior to completing the requested action and sending a final
   response. 1xx responses are terminated by the first empty line after
   the status-line (the empty line signaling the end of the header
   section).  Since HTTP/1.0 did not define any 1xx status codes, a
   server MUST NOT send a 1xx response to an HTTP/1.0 client.

   A client MUST be able to parse one or more 1xx responses received
   prior to a final response, even if the client does not expect one.  A
   user agent MAY ignore unexpected 1xx responses.

   A proxy MUST forward 1xx responses unless the proxy itself requested
   the generation of the 1xx response.  For example, if a proxy adds an
   "Expect: 100-continue" field when it forwards a request, then it need
   not forward the corresponding 100 (Continue) response(s).

```

1xx responses are very special, they cannot be the last response from the server (unless 101 Switching Protocols upgrades the protocol to another protocol like websocket or HTTP/2).  Since aiohttp accepts only one return value from the handler, the server side must check the status code of the response object. If the status code is 1xx, it must raise an exception and return a 500 response instead (as if there are no responses)

HTTP status code are defined in groups: 1xx - informational, 2xx - success, 3xx - redirection, 4xx - client error, 5xx - server error. Each group has special means and might be treated differently. They cannot swap with each other freely.

This is inherent to the way the cell-marking is currently implemented. 
The notion of anchor and cursor in the selection that @takluyver did implement fro multi-selection was removed, while it was one of the only decent way to actually track this kind of selection behavior without a lot of brittle heuristic. 

We might be able to track wether shift-J/K extend or contract the cell marking by looking at wether the cursor is at beginning or end of contiguous marking. Though this become ambiguous in some case where you have non-contiguous marking. 

We cannot either track wether shift was keeped pressed as you can do Shift-J,J,J leave you keyboard then again Shift-J,K,K and should end up with 2 cell selected. 

One of the correct way to implement that is to have the current "selection" be composed of a cursor (which is the one you move with J,K, and arrow keys) and an "Anchor". The anchor is *in general* always on the same cell as the cursor, except when you press shift, in which case the *Anchor*, stays on it's current cell. 

See this UI prototype where the anchor is explicitly marked by a Cyan background:

When shift is pressed, you can see that the cursor move independantly from the anchor. 
![anchor](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/335567/10983609/89d273e8-83c8-11e5-9475-f0220ad2314f.gif)

The default moving J/K always bring back the **anchor** and the **cursor** together.
 
I propose we reintroduce the the concept of **multiple selection** where all cells in between the anchor and the cursor are **selected** (and thus implicitly marked), then provide 2 shortcuts: 

  - mark all cell in selection 
  - unmark all cells in selection

For which `+` and `-` would be good shortcut candidates. 
This would make the process of multiple selection/marking much closer to all other  UI exposed to user. Plus this would allow Shift-J/K to deselect marked cells, and will allow most action to be done without an explicit marking. By not needing explicit marking we would also get rid of  lots of issues of having marked cells out of the screen. 

Thoughts ?

Discussion
==========

There's at least three approaches we could take in designing the signatures of
NailGun's helper methods:

1. Accept arguments individually and explicitly list each possible argument.
2. Accept arguments individually via `**kwargs`.
3. Accept arguments together as a dict.

As an example of each, consider `Subscription.upload`:

    def upload(self, content, organization, repository=None, synchronous=True):
    def upload(self, synchronous=True, **kwargs):
    def upload(self, payload, synchronous=True):

Which should be used? To answer that question, I use the following rubric:

* Can a user look at a method signature and its docstring and quickly start
  using the method?
* Can a user look at a method usage and intuit what is happening?
* Can a developer easily implement a method?
* Can a developer easily maintain a method over time?

Approach 1
----------

Recap:

> Accept arguments individually and explicitly list each possible argument.
>
>     def upload(self, content, organization, repository=None, synchronous=True):

### Signature & Docstring

Each possible argument is listed out. Furthermore, arguments required by the
server are also required by the method, and arguments not required by the server
are not required by the method. This helps.

There is no obvious distinction between the `synchronous` argument and other
arguments, which hurts.

### Usage

Consider these example method calls:

    upload(my_file_handle, my_org)
    upload(my_file_handle, my_org, my_repository)
    upload(my_file_handle, my_org, my_repository, False)

Each individual argument may be passed by position for conciseness or by keyword
for clarity. This helps.

The distinction between the `synchronous` argument and other arguments is not
obvious, which hurts.

The form of the payload sent to the server is not obvious. This hurts.

### Implementation

The developer must consult with the API documentation and discover where to
place each individual method in the request payload. This hurts.

The keywords required by the server may conflict with reserved words such as
"self", "synchronous", "type", "if", etc. This hurts.

### Maintenance

This method is *extremely* hard to maintain.

* Imagine that the structure of arguments accepted by the server changes. In
  this case, a version comparison must be done in the method and each possible
  layout must be listed.
* Imagine that the set of arguments accepted by the server changes. In this
  case, some painful hack must be performed, such as defining several nearly
  identical methods and binding one of them to an object at instantiation-time
  based on the server software version.

Approach 2
----------

Recap:

> 2. Accept arguments individually via `**kwargs`.
>
>     def upload(self, synchronous=True, **kwargs):

### Signature & Docstring

Possible arguments are not listed out. Instead, users must consult with the
docstring or API guide to see what arguments are acceptable. This hurts.

There is an obvious distinction between the `synchronous` argument and other
arguments, which helps.

`**kwargs` magic is used, which hurts.

### Usage

Consider these example method calls:

    upload(content=my_file_handle, organization_id=org.id)
    upload(
        content=my_file_handle,
        organization_id=org.id,
        repository_url=repo_url,
        synchronous=False,
    )

Each individual argument must be passed by keyword, which hurts.

The distinction between the `synchronous` argument is not obvious. Even worse,
all arguments are always passed by keyword, which means that arguments are
likely to be re-ordered, further blurring the distinction between `synchronous`
and other arguments. This hurts.

The form of the payload sent to the server is not obvious. This hurts.

### Implementation

The developer may blindly pass `**kwargs` to whatever `client.*` method is being
used, which helps.

The keywords required by the server may conflict with reserved words such as
"self", "synchronous", "type", "if", etc. This hurts.

### Maintenance

No changes are required if the set of arguments changes. No changes are required
if the semantics of arguments changes, such as which are required or optional.
This helps.

Clients may immediately use new features without any changes on our part. This
helps.

Approach 3
----------

Recap:

> 3. Accept arguments together as a dict.
>
>     def upload(self, payload, synchronous=True):

### Signature & Docstring

Possible arguments are not listed out. Instead, users must consult with the
docstring or API guide to see what arguments are acceptable. This hurts.

There is an obvious distinction between the `synchronous` argument and other
arguments, which helps.

### Usage

Consider these example method calls:

    upload({
        'content': my_handle,
        'organization_id': org.id,
        'repository_url': repo_url,
    })
    upload(payload, False)
    upload(payload, synchronous=False)

Each argument may be passed when the method is called or arguments may be
assembled in to a dict before-hand. Furthermore, all arguments may be passed
either by position or keyword. As a result, developers may choose whatever form
makes the most sense for their context. This helps.

The distinction between the `synchronous` argument and other arguments is
obvious. This helps.

The form of the payload sent to the server is obvious. This helps.

The methods provided by the mixins (`create*`, `read*`, `update*`, `delete*`,
`search*`) all list keywords as arguments, which is different from what is being
done here. That said, those methods are significantly different from helper
methods. Mixin methods use object state extensively, read payload arguments from
the object they are called on, and make use of call trees for easy overloading
and tweaking. In contrast, helper methods are one-off and make little use of
object state (mainly `path()`). As a result, letting the helper methods take a
different form from the mixin methods may actually be good.

### Implementation

The developer may blindly pass `payload` to whatever `client.*` method is being
used, which helps.

The keywords required by the server will never conflict with reserved words such
as "self", "synchronous", "type", "if", etc. This helps.

### Maintenance

No changes are required if the set of arguments changes. No changes are required
if the semantics of arguments changes, such as which are required or optional.
This helps.

Clients may immediately use new features without any changes on our part. This
helps.

Analysis
--------

Approach 1 is inadvisable. It will be a hassle to implement and a nightmare to
maintain.

Both approach 1 and 2 suffer from several issues resulting from their use of
keyword arguments:

* The `synchronous` argument is not clearly separate from arguments passed to
  the server. If any other arguments are added for some reason, they also are
  not clearly separated from arguments passed to the server.
* The arguments required by the server may conflict with reserved words: "self",
  "type", "synchronous", "def", etc. We work around this in the
  `Entity.__init__` method by clever use of sub-classing, which is doable. But
  working around those same issues in numerous once-off methods too will make
  implementation and maintenance harder.
* The form of the payload sent to the server is unclear. This is fine for the
  mixins — they provide `*_payload` methods, and an interested user may call one
  of those methods to see what is being sent to the server. But the convenience
  methods do not allow for that level of inspection, meaning that users will
  need to read the source code (or something even more laborious) to understand
  exactly what's going on.

One point discussed above is that using keyword arguments lets the helper
methods be similar to other methods in NailGun, such as `Entity.__init__` and
`EntityCreateMixin.create`. However, as discussed above:

> helper methods are one-off and make little use of object state (mainly
> `path()`). As a result, letting the helper methods take a different form from
> the mixin methods may actually be good.

Conclusion
----------

Use approach 3. All methods should have a signature in one of the following
forms:

    def helper_method(self, payload):
    def helper_method(self, payload, synchronous=True):
    def helper_method(self, payload, synchronous=True, …):

Be warned that these changes will produce *massive* breakage in Robottelo.

Miscellaneous Notes
-------------------

Use the shortest available path for performing any given task, where length is
measured in path segments. For example, given these two API calls:

    POST /katello/api/v2/organizations/:organization_id/subscriptions/upload
    POST /katello/api/v2/subscriptions/upload

Make the helper method call the latter path. Require that the user pass in an
organization ID in the arguments dict. I know, I know, it's inconvenient in many
cases. But being convenient means preventing the user from making certain types
of method calls. For example, imagine that the user wants to do this:

    my_subscription.upload({'organization_id': org.id, …})

What happens if `my_subscription.organization` is already set? Will that win
out, or will the argument passed in win out? What if no organization is set on
the object and none is passed in. Will an exception be raised when some bit of
"convenience" code tries to set the `organization_id` argument for us? What if a
quality engineer specifically wants to pass no organization ID. How can the
quality engineer do that?

It's much better to act dumb and let the user be explicit about what they want.
"When in doubt, leave it out!"

Assuming you're referring to the ACME account key pair as described in the [ACME spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-01#section-3), then yes, the manual authenticator needs access to your account private key. The reason for this is the ACME protocol requires that all requests are signed by the account private key ([source](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-01#section-5.1)).

If on the off chance you're referring to they private key that was used to sign the CSR that was provided with `--csr`, the LE client (regardless of authenticator) does not need access to that private key.

I hacked up a script plug-in (simpleHttp only) in a separate package, available at [git://git.silbe.org/letsencrypt-script-plugin.git](git://git.silbe.org/letsencrypt-script-plugin.git). It can be used like this:
`letsencrypt --authenticator letsencrypt-script-plugin:script --letsencrypt-script-plugin:script-auth-script custom-provision-script <other letsencrypt options>`

Apparently at least the Python argparse option parsing library has trouble parsing option values that may start with dashes (which can happen for tokens). So contrary to the syntax in the original report, no argument names are passed, only the options. Example:
`custom-provision-script simpleHttp .well-known/acme-challenge/token1 token1value example.com .well-known/acme-challenge/token2 token2value example.com`

This is enough to get me started using letsencrypt, but obviously lacks documentation and hasn't been polished, so I'm not proposing it for inclusion (⇒ no pull request). Feel free to use it as a basis for the official client, though.

As a precaution, I'm not familiar enough with the nginx configuration variation between different distributions, so the below comments are based on the information I have.

In apache land, the OS based conventions are historically very opinionated, and differ greatly (have a lot of added functionality) compared to the vanilla apache. Not only the vhost configuration directory layout, but the whole control script / module handling shebang. This requires us to have broader default configuration arrays for apache. Nginx doesn't have the historical burden, and has a lot clearer execution flow, without the additional distribution specific control scripts as the default way of handling things. 

For nginx, this looks like a robust way to find out the best location for the vhost files, if I'm not mistaken of the common configuration structures. 

It should be noted, that this will ignore the possible sites-available / sites-enabled - paradigm completely, and just write the new files to the sites-enabled (or whatever directory is parsed by the main configuration) instead. But at the same time, it allows the usage in different environments without having to guess defaults by LE client itself.

That being said, and if my assumptions about the nginx configuration variances are correct, this looks good to me.

Hi Yu-

Only some functions of hgvs + UTA will work with Ensembl transcripts. The limitation is really with UTA and not hgvs itself.

There are several issues. The first is that it's fairly hard to get the data I need for UTA from Ensembl, and therefore I don't do it very often. UTA is currently based on Ensembl 79. The second historical reason is that their fasta files used to have unversioned sequences. That means that the same (unversioned) accession was often associated with multiple distinct sequences over time, which violates an assumption in the code that accessions are associated with exactly one sequence. (Ensembl now versions accessions in their fasta files.) Finally, c_to_p requires a table of ENST-ENSP pairs, which is currently unpopulated for Ensembl. So, although hgvs works with Ensembl in principle (and has in the past), the combination is currently unsupported.

For the record, parsing and normalization work. And projecting variants between c and g works *but only for unversioned transcripts, because that's all that was available at the time*.

I'll close this issue in favor of biocommons/uta#207.

-Reece

> Row-major order is used in C/C++/Objective-C (for C-style arrays), PL/I,[3] Pascal, Speakeasy[citation needed], SAS,[4] and Rasdaman[5].

> Column-major order is used in Fortran, MATLAB,[6] GNU Octave, S-Plus,[7] R,[8] Julia,[9] Scilab, and Eigen[10].

from django.core.validators import MaxValueValidator, MinValueValidator
from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError


def NotEqualTo0(value):
    if value == 0:
        raise ValidationError('Word cannot be scored with 0 '
                              '(to avoid exceptions when exporting'
                              ' and importing the list into the database)')


class FrenchWord(models.Model):

    def __str__(self):
        return u"word: {} - score: {}".format(self.word, self.score)

    word = models.CharField(
        verbose_name="French word", unique=True, max_length=50)
    score = models.IntegerField(verbose_name="Score",
                                validators=[MaxValueValidator(2, "Score value greater than 2"),
                                            MinValueValidator(-2,
                                                              "Score value less than -2"),
                                            NotEqualTo0])


class ItalianWord(models.Model):

    def __str__(self):
        return u"word: {} - score: {}".format(self.word, self.score)

    word = models.CharField(
        verbose_name="Italian word", unique=True, max_length=50)
    score = models.IntegerField(verbose_name="Score",
                                validators=[MaxValueValidator(2, "Score value greater than 2"),

                                            NotEqualTo0])

@leplatrem Before proceeding any further I wanted to share my understanding of the requirements and the logic of how I have implement this so far.
The idea is that the user is expected to pass the path of the resource for us to trigger the web hook. For example: updating a bucket, collection, record would boil down to the following in the config file respectively:
```
kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids = /buckets/mybucketname
kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids = /buckets/mybucketname/collections/mycollection
kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids = /buckets/mybucketname/collections/mycollection/records/dasdad-addsa-saddaad
```
User is expected to specify the name of the resource they want to update.

In case of Create event, user is expected to pass the path of the parent. For example 
Creating a record:
`kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids  = /buckets/mybucketname/collections/mycollection`
Creating a collection:
`kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids  = /buckets/mybucketname`
Creating a bucket:
This is a special case since bucket doesn’t have a parent. In this case user can simply avoid adding `kinto.event_listeners.redis.resource_ids` line in the config and web hook would be triggered whenever a new bucket is created. I am happy to handle create bucket in a different manner if you have a suggestion.

    gamewin = games[0].stats.win

is what you want?

Hi,

the fine-tuned module will have to exported for later reuse, unfortunately we currently have no way of exporting a module from a Canned Estimator with feature_columns.

The simplest thing in this case is to write your own [custom estimator](https://www.tensorflow.org/guide/custom_estimators) and inside the model_fn, call the module directly, for example:
```
hub_module = hub.Module(spec)
hub.register_module_for_export(hub_module, "my_module")

embedding = hub_module(features["text"])
first_layer = tf.layers.dense(inputs=embedding, units=100, activation=tf.nn.relu)
second_layer = tf.layers.dense(inputs=first_layer, units=100, activation=tf.nn.relu)
logits = tf.layers.dense(inputs=second_layer, units=1, activation=None)
predictions = tf.sigmoid(logits)
```

Exporting from a custom estimator is well documented for example in this [test](https://github.com/tensorflow/hub/blob/2bd67f1df78c7d465254c3e21e96d8782b37d653/tensorflow_hub/estimator_test.py#L85).

I will keep this issue open until we make direct export from feature columns possible.

Yes, the intent of that logic is to only consider sending a Server Data Notification message if one or more of the listed conditions are true.

> 1) According to Thread 1.1 spec children can act as servers, hence I'm not clear why mMle.GetDeviceState() >= Mle::kDeviceStateRouter is needed.

OpenThread allows children to act as servers.  If the device is not currently a router, then at least one of the other conditions must be true for it to send a Server Data Notification message.

> 2) What is the reason to check for active router count?

If the active router count is above threshold, then this device (if it is a REED) will not attempt to become a router.  If the active router count is below threshold, then we want to delay sending the Server Data Notification message until after becoming a router or enough other routers have become active.

Best,

Agreed that having a `max_iter` parameter would be useful for Incremental. However, exposes a difficulty alluded to in https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/issues/200 and how `Incremental.get/set_params` works. It would be ambiguos whether `max_iter` should go to `Incremental.max_iter` or `Incremental.estimator.max_iter`.

The solution is to probably to have `Incremental` to specify a name when creating the `Incremental`.

See https://stackoverflow.com/a/35534452/1889400 for how `VotingClassifier` works.

So either

```python
inc = Incremental(SGDClassifier(), name='sgdclassifier')
inc = Incremental(('sgdclassifier', SGDClassifier())
```

In the latter case, we would have a `make_incremental` helper that works like `make_pipeline`.

Then in a context where you need to get / set params, it'd be

```python
param_grid = {
    'sgdclassifier__alpha': [0.1, 10.0],
    'sgdclassifier__max_iter': [1000, 2000],
    'max_iter': [1, 5],
}

clf = GridSearchCV(inc, param_grid)
```

Guys, any ideas on this? I'd be willing to finish it (if anything) but there's a few unclear points.

I think there are two issues here: the first is to get the class layout to pass through ``PyType_Ready``, which this commit accomplishes. The second is that this type of inheritance would require a rather different type of dispatch mechanism -- we'll have to keep track of two different instances -- one for CppType1, and one for CppType2. (A joint CppType1,CPPType2 type with virtual table and all will generally not exist, so we can't presume it to be there/instantiate it.) At runtime, it would be necessary to determine which of the instances should be used for dispatch and then forward to the appropriate type caster. It seems really complicated -- unless somebody has thoughts on how to do this without severely complicating the rest of pybind11, I'd say that it's probably not worth it.

Debugging this lead me to check both `views.get_channelstate_for_receiving` and `views.get_channelstate_open`. The former will return an empty list while the latter would return the list of open channels. Going back to the APIs, we have a parameter: `only_receiving_channels` which defaults to `True`. Meaning that we will only be triggering leaving a channels `for receiving` while other open channels can still be open.
The `leave` method of the `ConnectionManager` is documented with:

>         Note: By default we're just discarding all channels for which we haven't
>         received anything.  This potentially leaves deposits locked in channels after
>         `closing`. This is "safe" from an accounting point of view (deposits
>         can not be lost), but may still be undesirable from a liquidity point
>         of view (deposits will only be freed after manually closing or after
>         the partner closed the channel).
> 
>         If only_receiving is False then we close and settle all channels
>         irrespective of them having received transfers or not.
The confusing part here is that `views.get_channelstate_for_receiving` returns the list of channels from which we actually received transfers. So the first sentence of the `leave`'s method comment contradicts the logic implemented. 

Calling the leave API:
```
http --timeout 1000 DELETE http://localhost:5003/api/1/connections/0x0f114A1E9Db192502E7856309cc899952b3db1ED only_receiving_channels=false
```
Successfully triggered all channels to close. 

### Suggested solutions:
1. Make `only_receiving_channels` false by default
2. Do nothing and explicitly document the choice that the user has to make. However, we have to take into consideration that the UI doesn't prompt the user for selecting which channels to close when leaving the network, which brings us to the first point.

To document our discussion:

> Take into consideration the MediatedTransfer and CancelTransfer expiration in the netting contract

Is done and it is using the current block number. The current block number is the correct approach, the alternative of the closing block is wrong, here is why:

For a transaction using the nodes ABC, where Alice is the initiator, Bob is the only mediator and Charlie is the target, Bob needs the guarantee that if Charlie claims the lock form the BC channel then he will also be able to claim from the AB channel.

To achieve that each node has a reveal timeout configuration used as `expire(BC.lock) <= expire(AB.lock) - reveal_timeout`, the reveal timeout needs to be sufficient for Bob to learn the secret, call close, and then unlock. If we did use the *closing block* instead of the *current block* this doesnt hold.

```
Let's assume that all three nodes are using the defaults and these are:

settle_timeout = 15
reaveal_timeout = 5

then

expire(AB.lock) == 10
expire(BC.lock) == 5

if the *close block number* is used to check expiration, then:

An attacker that controls A and C:

- Calls BC.close at block 5
- Waits for block 11 and do not close I-M
- AB.unlock(lock) will work in this *example* since the closing block is used to validate the lock
- BC.unlock(lock) will not work in this *example* since the channel is not closed and the lock becomes expired
```

ie. if we did use the close block number we expand the lifetime of the lock up to settle_timeout blocks, and that is would not be correct.

## NoneType Error
Yes. I know why we are getting a `NoneType` error. 

The error occurred at `locator_as_index.intersection(base_index)`, which tries to find the intersection between `pd.Index(['2015-01-10 19:01:44'])` and the `df.index`. 

By default, 
```
In [2]: pd.Index(['2015-01-10 19:01:44'])
Out[2]: Index(['2015-01-10 19:01:44'], dtype='object')
```

What we need to do is try to convert it to datetime index *if `df.index` is datetime*:
```
In [3]: pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-01-10 19:01:44'])
Out[3]: DatetimeIndex(['2015-01-10 19:01:44'], dtype='datetime64[ns]', freq=None)
```
